textPlain: I mean, it's advice from someone on the internet, are there any studies to back it up? I floss after brushing, just because brushing removes a lot of particles. If I floss before, there is just so much more flossing I need to do to get everything out. I learned another thing recently, to use a proper brushing technique, I think it's the modified Bass technique. I also make sure to let the bristles go underneath the gum line.
I'm surprised that this isn't self-evident. Flossing dislodges stuff from between your teeth. Brushing beforehand means you don't have an opportunity to remove that stuff.
I thought the ideal order was brush, floss, mouthwash.
Is there empirical evidence that this order is more effective?
This is how I've always done it and I was blown away when I found out people do it backwards. It makes no logical sense.
Did they teach it the other way around somewhere? Here (Germany) I've been told floss before brush for as long as I can remember...
What about rinsing your toothbrush as you brush? Is there a min number of times you should do so? Just can I just wash it out when i am done?

I also rinse after flossing before brushing. Then I rinse again after brushing.I also rinse throughout the day whenever I can. It's not a routine for me as much as a habit along with generally always having plenty of water to keep things moving and clean.
I guess this goes for water pickers use too
I stubbornly floss every day and every yearly checkup my dentist tells me it does not help and recommends me to stop doing it.
When I get my teeth cleaned at the dentist, flossing is the last thing done. So I assume people internalize that

there are studies that show that flossing with the wrong technique doesn't do much. and it's meh on cavity prevention. what it does do is prevent gingival inflammation, which can be good for gum health, especially if you're prone to getting food caught in there.
Maybe your dentist just needs more business? Im half kidding but i once had a dentist that told me I had 4 cavities that needed to be fixed, but when I to a 2nd dentist, she spotted zero cavities
Some people do over floss and it can cause permanent gum damage. I assume that is a tiny fraction though.
Well based on the amount of shit I dig out even if I floss after, and it’s effect on my breath, ima keep doing it.
